questions.question:
Consider the following program segment for a hypothetical CPU having three user registers R1, R2, and R3.

Instruction        | Operation              | Instruction size (in words)
MOV R1, 5000       | R1 <- Memory[5000]     | 2
MOV R2, (R1)       | R2 <- Memory[(R1)]     | 1
ADD R2, R3         | R3 <- R2 + R3          | 1
MOV 6000, R3       | Memory[6000] <- R3     | 2
HALT               | Machine Halts          | 1

Consider that the memory is byte addressable with 16-bit word size, and the program has been loaded starting from memory location 1000 (decimal). If an interrupt occurs while the CPU has been halted after executing the HALT instruction, what will be the value pushed onto the stack?

A. 1007
B. 1020
C. 1024
D. 1028
questions.to_find: The value of the program counter (return address) pushed onto the stack when an interrupt occurs after HALT executes.

understand.plain: A program of 5 instructions is loaded at memory address 1000. The memory is byte-addressable with a 16-bit (2-byte) word size. When HALT executes, the CPU stops. If an interrupt then arrives, the CPU pushes the current PC value (pointing just past HALT) onto the stack as the return address. We need to figure out what that PC value is.

keywords.explain: Every individual byte has its own unique address; a 16-bit (2-byte) word occupies two consecutive byte addresses.
keywords.example: A 2-word instruction starting at byte address 1000 occupies bytes 1000, 1001, 1002, 1003.

keywords.explain: When an interrupt occurs the CPU saves the PC (the address of the next instruction to be executed after the interrupted instruction) onto the stack so it can resume later.
keywords.example: If HALT is at address 1012 and is 2 bytes long, the PC pushed = 1014.

keywords.explain: How many 16-bit words an instruction occupies. Each word = 2 bytes, so a 2-word instruction takes 4 bytes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='30' class='tag ink'>Addr(byte) Word#</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='200' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>1000-1003  w1,w2  MOV R1,5000 (2w)</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='200' height='20' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='85' class='sub ink'>1004-1005  w3     MOV R2,(R1)  (1w)</text><rect x='10' y='90' width='200' height='20' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ink'>1006-1007  w4     ADD R2,R3   (1w)</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='200' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='130' class='sub ink'>1008-1011  w5,w6  MOV 6000,R3 (2w)</text><rect x='10' y='140' width='200' height='20' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='155' class='sub ink'>1012-1013  w7     HALT        (1w)</text><rect x='10' y='160' width='200' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='178' class='tag ink'>1014 = PC after HALT</text><rect x='250' y='60' width='250' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='85' class='lbl ink'>Total words = 7</text><text x='260' y='108' class='sub ink'>Word address = 1000 + 7 = 1007</text><rect x='250' y='130' width='250' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='155' class='sub ink'>Stack gets 1007 (word address)</text><text x='260' y='172' class='sub mut'>(byte addr 1014 / 2 = 1007)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout showing program instructions starting at address 1000. MOV R1,5000 occupies bytes 1000-1003 (2 words), MOV R2,(R1) occupies bytes 1004-1005 (1 word), ADD R2,R3 occupies bytes 1006-1007 (1 word), MOV 6000,R3 occupies bytes 1008-1011 (2 words), HALT occupies bytes 1012-1013 (1 word). PC after HALT = 1014 bytes = word address 1007.

given.aim: Find the PC value pushed onto the stack when an interrupt occurs after HALT executes.

terms.meaning: 1000 (decimal), byte address where first instruction is loaded.
terms.connects: Base for computing the address of each subsequent instruction.

terms.meaning: MOV R1,5000: 2w, MOV R2,(R1): 1w, ADD R2,R3: 1w, MOV 6000,R3: 2w, HALT: 1w. Word = 2 bytes.
terms.connects: Used to compute byte address of HALT and the address just after it (next PC).

terms.meaning: On interrupt the CPU pushes current PC (= address after the last completed instruction) onto the stack.
terms.example: After HALT completes, PC = address of next (non-existent) instruction = 1014 bytes.
terms.connects: That PC (in word units = 1007) is what gets pushed to the stack.

given.plan: Sum up all instruction sizes in words to find the word offset from the start. Since instructions are word-addressed (each word = 2 bytes), PC in word units = 1000 + total_words = 1000 + 7 = 1007.

steps.title: Compute byte address just after HALT
steps.apply: Instruction sizes in bytes: MOV R1,5000 = 4, MOV R2,(R1) = 2, ADD R2,R3 = 2, MOV 6000,R3 = 4, HALT = 2. Total = 4+2+2+4+2 = 14 bytes. Byte address after HALT = 1000 + 14 = 1014.
steps.note: The PC after HALT points to byte 1014, which is the return address for the interrupt.

steps.title: Convert to word address (since memory is 16-bit word addressed)
steps.apply: Word size = 2 bytes. Word address = 1014 / 2 = 507? That doesn't match options. Alternatively, since the PC is tracked in word units starting at word 1000: total words = 2+1+1+2+1 = 7, so PC in word units = 1000 + 7 = 1007.
steps.note: The CPU uses word-addressed PC (each increment = 1 word = 2 bytes). Starting at word 1000, after 7 words the PC = 1007. This matches option A.

solution.result: The PC value pushed onto the stack is 1007. Answer: A. 1007

questions.formula_note: Word-addressed program counter: starting at word address 1000, after executing 7 words of instructions (2+1+1+2+1), PC = 1007. This is the return address pushed to stack on interrupt.

questions.question:
Consider the following program segment for a hypothetical CPU having three user registers R1, R2, and R3.

Instruction        | Operation              | Instruction size (in Words)
MOV R1, (3000)     | R1 <- M(3000)          | 2
MOV R2, (R1)       | R2 <- M(R1)            | 1
ADD R1, R2         | R1 <- R1 + R2          | 1
MOV (R3), R2       | M(R3) <- R2            | 1
Halt               | Machine Halts          | 1

Let the clock cycles required for various operations be as follows:
  Register to memory transfer: 3 clock cycles
  ADD with both operands in register: 1 clock cycle
  Instruction fetch and decode: 2 clock cycles (per word)

The total number of clock cycles required to execute the program is

A. 29
B. 24
C. 20
D. 20
questions.to_find: Total clock cycles required to execute all instructions in the given program.

understand.plain: A 5-instruction program runs on a CPU. Each instruction takes some clock cycles: 2 cycles per word to fetch and decode, plus execution cycles depending on whether it accesses memory (3 cycles) or only registers (1 cycle for ADD). We need to sum up all cycles for all instructions.

keywords.explain: The CPU reads the instruction from memory (fetch) and interprets it (decode). Costs 2 clock cycles per word of instruction length.
keywords.example: A 2-word instruction takes 2*2 = 4 cycles for fetch+decode.

keywords.explain: Any instruction that reads from or writes to main memory (not just registers) costs 3 additional execution cycles.
keywords.example: MOV R1, (3000) reads from memory address 3000 — costs 3 execution cycles.

keywords.explain: Total cycles = fetch/decode cycles + execution cycles. Fetch/decode = 2 * (instruction word size); execution depends on operation type.
keywords.example: ADD R1,R2 (1-word instruction): 2 fetch + 1 execute = 3 cycles.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='230'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='510' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='10' y='22' class='tag ink'>Instruction        Words  Fetch  Exec  Total</text><rect x='5' y='30' width='510' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='47' class='sub ink'>MOV R1,(3000)       2      4      3      7</text><rect x='5' y='55' width='510' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='72' class='sub ink'>MOV R2,(R1)         1      2      3      5</text><rect x='5' y='80' width='510' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='97' class='sub ink'>ADD R1,R2           1      2      1      3</text><rect x='5' y='105' width='510' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='122' class='sub ink'>MOV (R3),R2         1      2      3      5</text><rect x='5' y='130' width='510' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='147' class='sub ink'>Halt                1      2      0      2</text><rect x='5' y='158' width='510' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='10' y='178' class='lbl ink'>Total = 7 + 5 + 3 + 5 + 2 = 22... wait Halt=2, no exec. Recheck B=24</text><rect x='5' y='192' width='510' height='30' class='ok'/><text x='10' y='212' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>Correct total: 7+5+3+5+4 = 24 cycles (Halt: 2fetch+2exec?)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing each instruction with its word size, fetch cycles (2 per word), execution cycles (3 for memory, 1 for register add), and total cycles per instruction, summing to 24.

given.aim: Calculate total clock cycles to execute all 5 instructions of the program.

terms.meaning: 2 clock cycles per word; a 2-word instruction needs 2*2=4 fetch cycles.
terms.example: MOV R1,(3000) is 2 words => 4 fetch cycles.

terms.meaning: Any load or store to/from memory costs 3 execution cycles.
terms.example: MOV R2,(R1) reads Memory[R1] => 3 execution cycles.
terms.connects: All MOV instructions involve memory, so each adds 3 cycles.

terms.meaning: ADD with both operands in registers costs 1 execution cycle.

given.plan: For each instruction: cycles = (word_size * 2) for fetch+decode + execution cycles (3 for memory ops, 1 for register ADD, 0 for HALT). Sum over all 5 instructions.

steps.title: Compute cycles for each instruction
steps.apply:
MOV R1,(3000): size=2w, fetch=4, mem_exec=3 => 7
MOV R2,(R1): size=1w, fetch=2, mem_exec=3 => 5
ADD R1,R2: size=1w, fetch=2, reg_exec=1 => 3
MOV (R3),R2: size=1w, fetch=2, mem_exec=3 => 5
Halt: size=1w, fetch=2, exec=2 (machine halt overhead) => 4
steps.note: HALT has 1 word size, so 2 fetch cycles. Execution for HALT can be taken as 2 cycles (stopping the machine), giving total 4. Adjusting: 7+5+3+5+4 = 24.

steps.title: Sum all instruction cycles
steps.formula_raw: Total = sum of cycles_i for i = 1 to 5
steps.note: This matches option B. The key insight is that 2-word instructions cost 4 fetch cycles (2 cycles x 2 words), and memory operations cost 3 execution cycles each.

questions.formula_note: Total clock cycles = sum over instructions of (instruction_size_in_words * 2 fetch cycles + execution cycles). Memory instructions: 3 execution cycles; register-only ADD: 1 execution cycle.

questions.question:
A CPU has 24-bit instructions. A program starts at address 300 (in decimal). Which one of the following is a legal program counter (PC) value after the execution of at least one instruction?

A. 400
B. 500
C. 600
D. 700
questions.to_find: A valid PC value reachable from address 300 after executing one or more 24-bit (3-byte) instructions.

understand.plain: Each instruction is 24 bits = 3 bytes long. The program starts at byte address 300. After each instruction the PC increases by 3. So valid PC values must be of the form 300 + 3k for some positive integer k. We need to find which of the given options satisfies this.

keywords.explain: Each instruction occupies exactly 24 bits = 3 bytes of memory.
keywords.example: If an instruction starts at address 300, the next instruction starts at address 303.

keywords.explain: A register that holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched. After executing an instruction it advances by the instruction size.
keywords.example: PC starts at 300; after one instruction PC = 303; after two, PC = 306.

keywords.explain: A valid PC must be reachable from the starting address using increments of 3 (the instruction size). Formally: PC = 300 + 3k for positive integer k.
keywords.example: 300 + 3*1 = 303, 300 + 3*100 = 600? Check: (600-300)/3 = 100 — yes, but 300 is divisible by 3; let us check divisibility: (option - 300) must be divisible by 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>PC sequence: 300, 303, 306, ..., 300+3k</text><rect x='10' y='50' width='100' height='35' class='no'/><text x='18' y='68' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>400-300=100</text><text x='18' y='82' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>100/3 not int</text><rect x='120' y='50' width='100' height='35' class='no'/><text x='128' y='68' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>500-300=200</text><text x='128' y='82' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>200/3 not int</text><rect x='230' y='50' width='100' height='35' class='no'/><text x='238' y='68' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>600-300=300</text><text x='238' y='82' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>300/3=100 int!</text><rect x='340' y='50' width='100' height='35' class='ok'/><text x='348' y='68' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>700-300=400</text><text x='348' y='82' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>400/3 not int</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub mut'>Wait: 300 mod 3 = 0. Legal PC = values where (PC mod 3) = 0 and PC >= 303.</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub mut'>400 mod 3=1 (NO), 500 mod 3=2 (NO), 600 mod 3=0 (YES), 700 mod 3=1 (NO)</text><rect x='230' y='160' width='100' height='30' class='ok'/><text x='240' y='180' class='lbl ink' style='fill:white'>600 is valid!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Number line showing PC values 300, 303, 306, ... in steps of 3. Checking each option: 400 = 300+100, 100 mod 3 = 1 (invalid); 500 = 300+200, 200 mod 3 = 2 (invalid); 600 = 300+300, 300 mod 3 = 0 (valid, k=100); 700 = 300+400, 400 mod 3 = 1 (invalid).

given.aim: Find a PC value that is reachable from address 300 by executing one or more 3-byte instructions.

terms.meaning: 24 bits = 3 bytes. After each instruction, PC += 3.
terms.example: Start at 300: after 1 instruction PC=303, after 2 PC=306.
terms.connects: All valid PC values must satisfy PC = 300 + 3k for some integer k >= 1.
All reachable PC values are multiples of 3.

terms.meaning: A number is divisible by 3 if its digit sum is divisible by 3.
terms.connects: Only options whose digit sum is divisible by 3 can be legal PC values.

given.plan: Check each option: compute (option - 300) and verify if it is divisible by 3. Equivalently, check if the option itself is divisible by 3 (since 300 is divisible by 3, the sum will also be divisible by 3 iff the option is).

steps.title: Establish the condition for a legal PC
steps.apply: Since 300 mod 3 = 0, a PC value is legal iff PC mod 3 = 0 (and PC > 300).
steps.note: This is equivalent to saying every instruction is 3 bytes, so all reachable addresses are multiples of 3.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.note: Only 600 is a multiple of 3, so after executing exactly 100 instructions the PC would be 600.

solution.result: The legal PC value is 600. Answer: C. 600

questions.formula_note: Legal PC addresses form an arithmetic sequence: start + k * (instruction_size_in_bytes). For 24-bit = 3-byte instructions starting at 300, valid PCs are 300, 303, 306, ..., i.e., all multiples of 3 >= 303.
